chunks.text: Hypothermia has three stages. **Mild** (core 32-35°C / 90-95°F): violent shivering, cold limbs, slowed reactions. **Moderate** (28-32°C / 82-90°F): shivering stops, slurred speech, unsteady gait, drowsiness. **Severe** (<28°C / <82°F): loss of consciousness, irregular heartbeat, life-threatening. Cessation of shivering is a **deterioration signal, not recovery**.

The first step is always to **escape wind and rain — get into a tent, behind a rock, or under dense trees**. Wind chill makes heat loss several times faster than the air temperature suggests. Even above-freezing temperatures with strong wind and wet clothing can cause hypothermia.

Wet clothing conducts heat 25× faster than dry clothing. In a sheltered spot, **strip off all wet clothes immediately** and replace with dry layers. If no spare clothes, wring out existing ones before re-wearing and wrap with foil blanket or sleeping bag.

The reflective side of the foil blanket **faces the body** to reflect body heat back. Wrapping order: torso first, then head (head loses 30% of body heat), then limbs. Foil blanket is **insulation, not heating** — alone it is insufficient for severe hypothermia and needs to be combined with other warm layers.

If the patient is **conscious and able to swallow**, give warm sweetened drinks (hot cocoa, sugar water, sports drinks). **No alcohol**: alcohol dilates blood vessels and accelerates heat loss. **No caffeine**: it causes dehydration. Never feed an unconscious patient — high risk of aspiration.

Do not **apply direct heat or open fire to extremities** — peripheral vasodilation sends cold blood back to the heart, potentially causing fatal arrhythmia. Do not **vigorously rub or massage limbs** — same reason. Do not **let the patient sleep alone**; maintain conversation to keep them alert.

Call SOS the moment the patient shows **moderate hypothermia signs** (shivering stops, slurred speech, drowsy). In wilderness, moderate hypothermia can progress to severe within 1-2 hours and requires medical equipment to reverse. While waiting, **two rescuers performing skin-to-skin warming** of the patient's torso is an effective emergency measure.

After a venomous snake bite, **stay calm and stop all activity**. Vigorous movement raises heart rate and accelerates venom spread through the bloodstream. Sit or lie down, keep the bite **below heart level** (unless the bite is on the head or neck).

The old practices of "tourniquet", "cut the wound and suck out venom", "ice packing", "alcohol" are **all wrong or harmful**, and modern first-aid explicitly opposes them. Tight tourniquets cause limb necrosis by cutting blood flow. Incising the wound increases infection risk. Mouth suction poisons the rescuer.

The correct method is **pressure immobilisation**: wrap an elastic bandage or cloth firmly but **uniformly** around the bite and the entire limb above it (band width about 5 cm, snug enough to fit one finger underneath). This slows lymphatic flow without blocking arterial blood. Splint the whole limb with sticks to minimize movement.
Immediately remove rings, watches, tight sleeves near the bite. **Swelling worsens within minutes** and items become impossible to remove later, potentially cutting off circulation.

If safe, photograph the snake (do NOT risk a second bite). Otherwise note **color, pattern, head shape (triangular or oval), length**. This information helps medical staff identify the species and choose the correct antivenom. Common venomous snakes in Taiwan: pit viper (龜殼花), bamboo snake (赤尾青竹絲), banded krait (雨傘節), cobra (眼鏡蛇), Russell's viper (鎖鏈蛇), hundred-pacer (百步蛇).

After pressure immobilisation, **call SOS immediately** with location, bite time, and snake description. While waiting, keep the patient warm, calm, and monitor breathing and consciousness. If breathing fails, begin CPR.

Antivenom is most effective when given early but **must be administered in a hospital** because it can cause allergic reactions. Never inject any medication in the field. The golden window from bite to hospital is within 4 hours.

The moment you realize you are lost, execute STOP: **Stop, Think, Observe, Plan**. Studies show lost hikers who keep moving expand their search radius, exhaust energy, and reduce their chance of being found. Stop and take 10 deep breaths to let panic subside.

The most dangerous reaction is to **"go downhill" or "follow a stream"**. Mountain terrain in Taiwan and similar regions is fragmented — streams flow into waterfalls and gorges with extremely high fatality rates. The correct response is to **stay put** and let rescuers come to you, not chase you.

Recall when you last confirmed you were on the trail. Observe terrain features: slope direction, vegetation, any man-made markers (trail tape, paint marks, plastic markers). If you can see the silhouette of nearby peaks, this helps rescuers triangulate.

If you do not expect rescue before dark, set up shelter. Choose a **wind-blocked, slightly elevated, no falling-rock location**. Use branches, leaves, and terrain to build a wind wall. Lay leaves and bark on the ground to insulate from cold ground (ground conducts heat far faster than air does).

A fire provides **warmth** and acts as a **rescue signal**. During the day, use wet leaves or pine needles to produce **thick white smoke** (high moisture, dense smoke). At night, keep visible **flames**. Three fires in a triangle or in a line is the international distress signal. Gather sufficient fuel before nightfall so it does not burn out at 3 AM.

Shouting wears out vocal cords within 30 minutes and carries only a short distance. A **whistle can be blown hundreds of times and pierces forest**. The international distress signal is **3 blasts per minute** (~5 sec apart), pause 1 minute, repeat. Any response of 3 blasts in a group means rescuers are approaching.

Mobile phones often have no signal in mountains. **Close all apps, turn off Bluetooth, enable airplane mode** to save battery. Every 1-2 hours, climb to a higher open spot and turn the phone back on to attempt a transmission. **SMS uses less power and succeeds with weaker signal than voice** (SMS retransmits automatically). When signal is available, dial 911 / 112 with GPS coordinates (iOS / Android show coordinates even without network).

Priority order: **mobile call 911 > satellite SOS (iPhone 14+ Emergency SOS) > large signal fire > 3-whistle blasts > mirror flash (to helicopters)**. Never give up active signaling — rescue teams investigate any signal aggressively.
失溫分三級。**輕度**(體溫 32-35°C):劇烈發抖、四肢冰冷、反應變慢。**中度**(28-32°C):停止發抖、口齒不清、步態不穩、嗜睡。**重度**(<28°C):意識喪失、心律不整、生命危險。停止發抖是惡化信號,**不是好轉**。
第一步永遠是**脫離風雨、進入避難處**:帳篷、岩石背風面、樹叢內。風冷效應(wind chill)讓散熱速度比氣溫顯示的快數倍,即使氣溫零度以上,強風加濕衣仍可導致失溫。
濕衣服導熱率是乾衣服的 25 倍。在能避風的位置**立刻脫掉所有濕衣物**,換上乾的保暖層。若沒備用衣物,擰乾現有衣物再穿,並用鋁箔毯或睡袋包裹外層。
鋁箔毯的反射面**朝向身體**才能反射體熱回去。包裹順序:先包軀幹再包頭部(頭部散熱占全身 30%),最後手腳。鋁箔毯是「保溫」不是「加熱」,單獨使用對重度失溫不夠,需配合其他保暖層。
患者**意識清醒且能吞嚥**時,可給溫熱含糖飲料(熱可可、糖水、運動飲料)。**禁止給酒精**:酒精造成血管擴張,反而加速散熱。**禁止給咖啡因**:利尿造成脫水。意識不清者絕對不可餵食,有嗆入肺部窒息風險。
不要**直接火烤**(末梢血管擴張會把冷血送回心臟,可能造成心律不整猝死)。不要**用力按摩或搓揉手腳**(同樣原因)。不要讓患者**單獨睡著**,持續對話保持清醒。
只要患者**出現中度失溫症狀**(停止發抖、口齒不清、嗜睡)就應立即 SOS。野外環境下中度失溫可在 1-2 小時內惡化為重度,需要醫療設備才能逆轉。在等待救援期間,**兩位施救者貼身保暖**患者軀幹是有效的緊急措施(skin-to-skin 體溫傳遞)。
被毒蛇咬到後,**保持冷靜並停止活動**是最重要的一步。劇烈活動會加速心跳,讓蛇毒透過血液循環擴散更快。坐下或躺下,讓被咬部位**低於心臟**(若被咬的是頭頸部除外)。

chunks.h2: 不要做這些事(常見錯誤)
chunks.text: 過去常見的「結紮」「切開傷口吸吮」「用嘴吸毒液」「冰敷」「喝酒」**全都是錯誤或有害的做法**,現代急救已明確反對。緊綁止血帶會阻斷血流造成肢體壞死,切開傷口會增加感染機會,吸吮會讓施救者中毒。
正確的做法是「壓力固定法」:用彈性繃帶或衣物,在傷口及其上方部位**寬鬆均勻**纏繞(寬度約 5 公分,可塞入一指鬆緊度),讓淋巴回流減慢但不阻斷動脈血流。整條肢體再用木棍 / 樹枝固定,減少活動。
被咬部位附近的戒指、手錶、緊身衣袖必須**立即移除**。蛇毒造成的腫脹會在數十分鐘內加劇,屆時很難脫下,可能造成局部血流阻斷。
若安全可拍照(切勿冒險靠近),或記下蛇的**體色、花紋、頭型(三角形或橢圓)、長度**。這些資訊有助於醫療人員判定蛇種,選用對應的抗蛇毒血清。台灣常見的毒蛇有六種:龜殼花、赤尾青竹絲、雨傘節、眼鏡蛇、百步蛇、鎖鏈蛇。
完成壓力固定後,**立即 SOS 求救**並回報定位、被咬時間、蛇外觀。等待救援期間保持患者溫暖、安撫情緒、持續觀察呼吸與意識。若患者出現呼吸困難、意識模糊、大量流血,立即進行 CPR 並準備施救。
抗蛇毒血清越早施打越有效,但**必須在醫院或專業醫療設施內施打**,因為可能引發過敏反應。野外不要自行注射任何藥物。從被咬到送醫的黃金時間是 4 小時內。
迷路第一刻請立刻執行 STOP:**Stop(停止)、Think(思考)、Observe(觀察)、Plan(計畫)**。研究顯示迷路者繼續移動會擴大搜尋範圍、增加體力消耗、降低被尋獲機率。停下來深呼吸 10 次,讓恐慌減退。
最危險的反應是**「往下走」或「沿溪流走」**。台灣山區地形破碎,溪流會匯入瀑布、峽谷,沿溪下行死亡率極高。正確做法是**停在原地**,讓救援者向你靠近,而不是追著你跑。
回憶上一次確認在「路上」是多久之前。觀察周圍地形特徵:坡向、植被、有無人工標記(路條、油漆記號、塑膠桶)。若能看到山頭輪廓,可協助救援者三角定位。
預期天黑前無法獲救就要紮營。選**背風、地勢稍高、附近無落石**的位置。利用樹枝、樹葉、地形構築擋風牆。地面鋪設葉子、樹皮隔絕地面寒氣(地面散熱比氣溫散熱快數倍)。
生火**取暖**並**作為救援信號**。白天用濕葉子或松枝製造**白煙**(濕度大、煙粗),夜間維持**火焰可見**。火堆排成三角形或三個並排的火堆是國際求救信號。生火前先準備充足燃料,以免半夜熄滅。
人聲喊叫 30 分鐘後聲帶就壞,且傳播距離有限。**哨子可吹數百次不累,聲音穿透樹林**。國際求救信號是**每分鐘吹 3 聲**(間隔約 5 秒),停 1 分鐘再重複。聽到回應(任何 3 聲一組的聲音)就是救援者在靠近。
手機在山區常無訊號,**關閉所有 app 與藍牙、開啟飛航模式**省電。每隔 1-2 小時、爬到較高開闊處再開機嘗試發訊。**簡訊比通話省電且容易成功**(訊號弱時 SMS 可重傳)。若有訊號,撥 119 並提供 GPS 座標(iOS / Android 內建定位功能,即使無網路也能顯示座標)。
求救優先序:**手機 119 > 衛星訊號(iPhone 14+ 緊急衛星)> 大型信號火 > 哨子三聲 > 鏡子反射陽光(對直升機)**。任何時候不要放棄主動發訊,救援隊聽到任何信號都會積極搜尋。
